paper_metadata.title: Public support for carbon pricing policies and revenue recycling options: a systematic review and meta-analysis of the survey literature
paper_metadata.authors: Farah Mohammadzadeh Valencia, Cornelia Mohren, Anjali Ramakrishnan, Marlene Merchert, Jan C. Minx, Jan Christoph Steckel
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: 26 countries across Africa, Asia and Pacific, Europe and West Asia, Latin America and the Caribbean, and North America
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis of survey evidence on public support for carbon taxes and fuel taxes, especially how revenue recycling options affect acceptability.

determinants.definition: Revenues earmarked for climate-friendly or environmental projects instead of unspecified use or public finance.
determinants.mechanism: May increase perceived effectiveness and issue-linkage by tying the tax to visible environmental benefits.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Meta-regression of change in public support for carbon pricing schemes, baseline: public finance.
determinants.evidence_quote: only those schemes for environmental purposes (green spending) are associated with a statistically significant positive effect
determinants.notes: Only revenue recycling option statistically significant in the main logit model.

determinants.definition: Revenues redistributed to low-income or otherwise affected households or groups.
determinants.mechanism: May address distributional fairness and compensate groups expected to bear higher costs.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Meta-regression of change in public support for carbon pricing schemes, baseline: public finance.
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Positive point estimate but not statistically significant.

determinants.definition: Equal lump-sum redistribution of revenues to all households or the entire population.
determinants.mechanism: May reduce net burden but could be less attractive if viewed as insufficiently targeted or inequitable.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Meta-regression of change in public support for carbon pricing schemes, baseline: public finance.
determinants.evidence_quote: uniform cash transfers (−0.60; standard error 0.86; p > 10%)
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Negative point estimate but not statistically significant.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: tax cuts – income, labour, and consumption
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Tax cuts for income, labour, or consumption taxes
determinants.definition: Revenue used to reduce personal income, labour, or consumption taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Can offset tax burden and improve net welfare, but effect is imprecise in the meta-analysis.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Meta-regression of change in public support for carbon pricing schemes, baseline: public finance.
determinants.evidence_quote: Tax cuts – income, labour, and consumption ... 0.69 ... p > 10%
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Positive point estimate but not statistically significant.

determinants.definition: Revenue used to lower taxes paid by firms.
determinants.mechanism: May increase acceptability if respondents favor business relief, but estimated effect is imprecise.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Meta-regression of change in public support for carbon pricing schemes, baseline: public finance.
determinants.exclusion_reason_if_not_main: Negative point estimate but not statistically significant.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Carbon and environmental tax versus fossil-fuel subsidy reform
determinants.definition: Type of policy compared against fossil-fuel subsidy reform in the meta-regression.
determinants.mechanism: Captures whether support differs for direct environmental taxes relative to subsidy removal policies.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Policy-type moderator in the meta-regression; baseline: fossil fuel subsidy reform.
determinants.evidence_quote: Carbon and environmental tax (baseline: Fossil fuel subsidy reform) 1.2 (1.12)
determinants.notes: Policy-type moderator, not a revenue recycling option.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Existing carbon pricing policy in place
determinants.definition: Whether a carbon pricing policy already existed in the country at the time of the survey.
determinants.mechanism: May reflect prior policy familiarity or learning effects that shape support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Country context moderator in the meta-regression.
determinants.evidence_quote: Carbon pricing policy in place: Yes (baseline: No) 0.73 (0.70)

determinants.definition: Survey conducted in Africa relative to North America.
determinants.mechanism: Captures regional political-economic context and institutional conditions associated with stronger support changes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Regional fixed effect in the meta-regression; baseline: North America.

determinants.definition: Survey conducted in Asia and Pacific relative to North America.
determinants.mechanism: Captures regional context associated with higher support change than the North American baseline.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Regional fixed effect in the meta-regression; baseline: North America.

determinants.definition: Survey conducted in Latin America and the Caribbean relative to North America.
determinants.mechanism: Captures regional context associated with higher support change than the North American baseline.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Regional fixed effect in the meta-regression; baseline: North America.
determinants.evidence_quote: Latin America and Caribbean (baseline: North America) 2.67***
determinants.notes: Evidence base in this region is limited.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Europe and West Asia regional effect
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Positive but not statistically significant; omitted to prioritize clearer determinants.
